candidate_problems.text: Can computational irreducibility do explanatory work beyond the standard Past Hypothesis, or is it merely an epistemic/practical gloss on low-entropy initial conditions? The key unresolved tension is whether irreducibility is necessary for the arrow of time, or whether the conjecture redescribes the usual statistical-mechanical asymmetry without adding a distinct physical principle or testable consequence.

What precise notion of 'computational irreducibility' is relevant for physical time-asymmetry, and how could it be defined or measured in actual dynamical systems? The conjecture depends on tractability, compression, and lack of shortcuts, but it is unclear whether these are observer-relative complexity notions, objective features of histories, or properties of coarse-grained descriptions.

How does the conjecture reconcile the claim that the future lacks generated correlations with block-universe, path-integral, and time-symmetric boundary-condition formulations of physics in which entire histories are treated globally? The open question is whether the proposed arrow is genuinely ontological for embedded agents or only a perspectival asymmetry arising from record formation and inference constraints.
